skill.name: binary-re-synthesis
skill.description: Create structured binary reverse engineering reports from collected triage, static, and dynamic analysis evidence.
skill.summary: Summarize binary analysis findings with evidence-backed conclusions and report templates.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged shell syntax, network indicators, weak cryptography terms, log paths, and reconnaissance language. Review found these are Markdown examples for binary-analysis reports, not executable code or operational instructions. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malicious execution behavior was found.

low_findings.title: External Command Matches Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The backtick detections are fenced Markdown examples and report templates. They do not invoke a shell, run Ruby code, or define executable behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines are Markdown fence boundaries and sample report blocks. No executable Ruby, shell command, script file, or command dispatch is present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded IP Address Is Sample Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The IP address appears in a correlation example showing how to document observed network behavior in a report.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside a narrative example and is not used as a destination by skill code. No network request mechanism exists in the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Terms Are Documentation Labels
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The SHA256, MD5, DES, and hash references are report fields or checklist examples. They do not implement cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references are plain text in templates and examples. There is no cryptographic library call, hashing implementation, or security-sensitive crypto choice.

low_findings.title: Log File Deletion Finding Is A Log Path Example
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged log reference describes an example binary writing a log file. It does not delete, truncate, or hide logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is part of a sample final report entry and states that logging writes to a path. No deletion language or executable command is present.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Match Is Skill Scope Text
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The description names binary analysis phases and reporting tasks. It does not collect host information or perform reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text defines the skill purpose and contains no host probing instructions. The broader domain is dual use, so residual risk is low rather than safe.

content.value_statement: Binary analysis often produces scattered notes, hypotheses, and tool outputs. This skill turns those materials into clear reports with traceable evidence.
actual_capabilities: Organizes triage, static, and dynamic analysis facts into a report structure.
Maps hypotheses to confirmed, refuted, uncertain, or unvalidated statuses.
Connects related evidence across functions, strings, syscalls, files, and network observations.
Builds capability summaries for network, file system, cryptography, and execution behavior.
Creates human-readable Markdown report templates and evidence logs.
Suggests next analysis steps and security review recommendations.
limitations: It does not reverse engineer binaries by itself.
It depends on accurate findings from earlier analysis phases.
It cannot verify behavior that was not observed or documented.
It may require analyst review for ambiguous or incomplete evidence.

use_cases.title: Prepare a handoff report
use_cases.description: Convert analysis notes into a structured document for another security engineer.

use_cases.title: Summarize malware behavior
use_cases.description: Group observed capabilities, external communications, and open questions into a concise finding set.

use_cases.title: Document firmware review results
use_cases.description: Record binary purpose, confidence, file access, network behavior, and follow-up checks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize these binary analysis notes into an executive summary, confirmed findings, and remaining unknowns.

prompt_templates.title: Validate hypotheses
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these hypotheses against the available evidence. Mark each one as confirmed, refuted, uncertain, or unvalidated.

prompt_templates.title: Build a capability map
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a capability summary from these static and dynamic findings. Include confidence levels and evidence references.

prompt_templates.title: Produce a full analysis report
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a complete binary analysis report with artifact details, behavior, evidence log, recommendations, and open questions.

output_examples.input: Notes from triage, static analysis, and dynamic testing for an unknown Linux binary.
output_examples.output: A structured report with an executive summary, artifact table, confirmed capabilities, evidence log, and next steps.

output_examples.input: A list of hypotheses about network behavior and file system access.
output_examples.output: A validation table showing which hypotheses are supported, contradicted, unresolved, or lacking evidence.

output_examples.input: Findings from a potentially malicious sample that need sharing with stakeholders.
output_examples.output: A readable threat-focused summary with behavior, confidence, indicators, recommendations, and unresolved questions.

best_practices: Provide source evidence for every conclusion before asking for synthesis.
Separate confirmed observations from inferred behavior and speculation.
Keep raw tool output in appendices and summarize the important findings.
anti_patterns: Do not ask it to invent missing evidence or assign confidence without support.
Do not mix unrelated binaries in one report unless comparing them directly.
Do not treat generated reports as final without analyst review.

faq.question: Does this skill analyze binary files directly?
faq.answer: No. It summarizes and organizes findings collected from other analysis steps.

faq.question: Can it write malware reports?
faq.answer: Yes. It can document observed behavior, evidence, confidence, and recommendations for suspicious binaries.

faq.question: What inputs work best?
faq.answer: Use notes with file hashes, architecture, functions, strings, syscalls, network observations, and analyst hypotheses.

faq.question: Does it require a specific toolchain?
faq.answer: No. It can synthesize findings from many tools if the evidence is clearly described.

faq.question: Can it export structured formats?
faq.answer: It provides guidance for structured summaries, but final format support depends on the host assistant.

faq.question: Is the content safe for marketplace use?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation focused. It contains dual-use security examples but no executable analysis tooling.
